just have to make sure that you are scrubbing the toilets so that when you tell somebody to scrub the toilets, they have the proper tools to scrub the toilets, not to get too metaphorical, but it's really important.

The way the basic structure is, is that we have directors which oversee like a group of pods and then the managing editors, like the technical managing editor role that we're talking about today.
They, they would be sort of the day-to-day internal external bridge for the clients.

So talking to the clients, making sure that, you know, their content strategy is still what they said it was at the beginning of an engagement, evolving that strategy over time, talking about new opportunities, just keeping them engaged.
Because obviously with any client, they're always on the way out.
Every client is always on the way out.
That's the way I look at it.
And if you build loops.
Keep them engaged and interested and view you as a productive, not just a reactive member of the team.
Then you're able to get a much longer engagement under your belt and keep them excited.
And frankly, we learn faster when we do more stuff for clients.
So it's all good.
And that's the ME's job in part.
And then the other part is just keeping the spice flowing from the content manager side.
We view the stack like this.
The pipelines are the writers.
The content managers manage the pipelines.
So that's why manager is in their name.
They're not managing people.
They're managing pipelines.
Then the ME will manage a handful of CMs working on a handful of clients.
And they don't manage them on a person basis, but they're managing their flow.
And they're helping them to articulate and understand where that breakdown is.
So if the content manager says, oh man, it's taking me like an hour to get these into shape.
It's like, that's it.
Just thank you for bringing that up.
Why?
Like, what are you doing?
And then articulating that, helping them to articulate it because that is a skill that comes at a certain level.

You know, they're good writers.
They've done solid marketing copy for agencies or for internal teams for years and years.
That's great.
But as you probably well know, not all writers are good at explaining how they do, what they do.

Stuff goes in, stuff comes out.
Can they explain how they did it?

Right.

And those.
Folks usually are better at articulation.

But what we have found is that the ME role needs to have a balance of client relationship management on an ongoing basis, technical aptitude with AI tooling and workflows, because that's what our tools are built with.
So unless you know how to speak to them and know the ways in which they will willfully misinterpret what you tell them, it can become frustrating because you're unable to properly articulate.
And then a bit of process management when it comes to keeping CMs running on time, pulling out of them what is going on.

I have found, as you probably have found, that when you're working with people at like a, let's call it a junior level or a level where they have deliverables and they're working as an IC, they often are reluctant to tell you where their blockers are and that they're having difficulties because they view that as, oh, no, I'm saying I'm bad at my job.
And so it's sometimes an effort to say, hey, I'm here to help.
Like, I'm here to figure out.
And out why you are taking much longer than you would wish to.
And then it is honestly a direct line from there to our product being better.
So it's much more beneficial to everybody involved, including you, if we're able to pull out what is missing and then build it into the pipeline.

research into their product, the brand, their ICP, ICP pain points, ICP goals.

initially, absolutely not.

So it is like a, hey, what if there was never any other way to write and you had to write with LLMs, you know, how would you articulate the process of writing to them?
And they already know a lot about the English language.
They are language models, right?
At their core.

So they have a lot of ideas about how words go together, how they're pattern matched to sort of appear in a certain order, etc.
They also have a tendency to take all of the worst aspects.
of the written language and couple them with other worst aspects of the language.
So they do have a lot of opportunity for improvement.
And a good amount of what we have built are Rails, for lack of a better term.
They're guidelines, Rails, funnels.
Rails is like a loaded term because it implies a single track.
And you cannot do that if you want to allow creativity and allow flexibility and malleability to the content.
Because otherwise, you're just producing formulaic content, purely formulaic, where it'll do certain sections over and over.
It'll do certain kind of bullet points that are maybe expanded into paragraphs over and over, etc.
You need to let it wander a bit.
It's just having outer bounds on that wandering.
And then, once it wanders, you can come in with a scalpel.
And say,

Hey, there's a lot of compound sentences in here.

Let's bring that down.
There are, of course, all the punctuation stuff that people care about.

Another huge thing.
Are these things actually true?

Company context, which is what's the company, what are its products, discovered much in the same way that you have done discovery before, SME interviews, kickoff calls, chats, provisions of internal documentation.

We just go the extra mile to process them with researchers and AI into our knowledge base or into artifacts so that we make sure that we have captured an enormous amount of internal context that if they hired a staff writer and that staff writer spent months poring over all of their internal documentation, perhaps they could reach, achieve that level of mastery.
It'd be like if the CEO had no other job, his only job was to write, and then he was also a good writer, right?

So I know because I've read all of their stuff.

And then the third persona, which is probably the most vital one, is the writing guidelines.
And the writing guidelines, I view it as an entity.
It really can be broken up into component parts, but it are things like general philosophy about the way that you write for that audience about this company.
